text: The surgeon performs an open haemorrhoidectomy on a 55-year-old patient in lithotomy position with grade four circular haemorrhoids. The right anterior hemorrhoidal complex is grasped with an Alice clamp. A triangular incision is made at the base of the anoderm using a monopolar electrosurgery device, extending to the external anal margin. Downward traction is applied to the hemorrhoid, followed by careful dissection between the external hemorrhoid plexus and external anal sphincter fibers towards the pedicle. At the transition zone, the anal mucosa and submucosa are divided with ligature towards the pulse pedicle. The dissection plane lies between the internal anal sphincter fibers and internal hemorrhoidal plexus tissue. Ligature is applied on both sides of the hemorrhoid cushion to cut mucosa and submucosa until reaching the pedicle, ensuring the internal sphincter muscles remain intact. The pedicle is divided with the device's jaws applied perpendicular.

The surgeon assesses the defect size, noting the maximum width of the wound in the distal part is two centimeters. The excision line of the anoderm at the base of the left lateral complex is marked, while preserving the mucocutaneous bridge between the two wounds. The surgeon uses his index finger to continuously control the bridge, ensuring the excisional wound size is equal to or less than the bridge width to prevent stricture.

The surgeon performs excision of the right posterior hemorrhoidal pile using monopolar electrosurgery followed by a ligature small jaw open sealer. The hemorrhoidal pile is dissected from the external and internal sphincters, achieving bloodless and precise excision without sutures in the anal canal. The jaws of the device are applied perpendicular to the pedicle to complete the excision. The wound is inspected for satisfactory hemostasis.

The removed hemorrhoidal cushion measures approximately two centimeters, with the mucocutaneous bridge also measuring two centimeters in width. Pre-operative and post-operative images show the surgical site, and at one month post-operation, the wound has healed well without signs of prolapse or anal bleeding.
